description: WRAP v0 conformance vectors (14-conformance.md §15). Normative and take precedence over the prose wherever the two disagree (§15.1). See conformance/README.md for the value-typing convention, key-derivation convention, and how to drive these vectors against an implementation.

value_typing_convention.uint: CBOR major type 0 (unsigned integer). v is a JSON number, always >= 0.
value_typing_convention.int: CBOR major type 0 or 1, chosen by sign. v is a JSON number, may be negative.
value_typing_convention.float64: CBOR major type 7, IEEE 754. v is a JSON number for ordinary values; special values (NaN, +-Infinity, -0.0) use {"t":"float64","special":"nan"|"inf"|"neg_inf"|"neg_zero"} instead of v.
value_typing_convention.bool: CBOR major type 7 (0xf4/0xf5). v is a JSON boolean.
value_typing_convention.tstr: CBOR major type 3 (UTF-8 text string). v is a JSON string.
value_typing_convention.bstr: CBOR major type 2 (byte string). v is a lowercase hex string (no 0x prefix), '' for empty.
value_typing_convention.array: CBOR major type 4 (definite-length array). v is a JSON array of typed values.
value_typing_convention.map: CBOR major type 5 (definite-length map) with UNSIGNED-INTEGER keys. v is a JSON object whose keys are decimal strings of the uint key, values are typed.
value_typing_convention.refmap: The one WRAP map with TEXT-string keys instead of uint keys — `WorkOrder.refs` (key 14) only (§3.3). v is a plain JSON object of string:string.

wo1.author: issuer

7.v: Deliver 2 bags of cement to 12 River Rd

8.v: Leave with site foreman if no answer

wo1.note: Full WorkOrder exercising every documented field (§3.3) and the Place/Window/Compensation value types (§3.9-3.11).

wo2_unknown_field.author: issuer
wo2_unknown_field.note: Minimal WorkOrder plus ONE field at key 20 — inside the 16-31 core-reserved range (§4.5) — that this document does not define. Stands in for a future core field an old implementation must not understand, but MUST preserve losslessly on decode->re-encode.

offer1.author: issuer
offer1.note: Offer referencing wo1, open-bid mode, to a Pool Principal.

bid1.author: performer
bid1.note: Bid answering offer1, naming a quote and an ETA.

bid_any_principal.author: imposter
bid_any_principal.note: A second bid on the SAME offer, authored by a totally unrelated third party (`imposter`). Pins §5.5's `Bid: any Principal the offer's pool admits` — pool admission is a social/out-of-band concern (§8.4), so at the wire-verification layer this MUST decode and signature-verify exactly like bid1, with no authorship rejection.

assignment_legit.author: issuer
assignment_legit.note: The WorkOrder's own issuer assigns wo1 to `performer`. The only authorship-valid Assignment in this file.

assignment_forged_by_performer.author: performer
assignment_forged_by_performer.note: The PERFORMER signs their own Assignment to themselves for wo1. Cryptographically valid (correctly signed, canonically encoded, correct id) — decodes and signature-verifies cleanly — but violates the authorship rule (§3.6, §5.5): only wo1's issuer may author an Assignment for wo1. MUST be rejected with ERR_NOT_ISSUER at the authorship-check layer, not the decode layer.

assignment_forged_by_third_party.author: imposter
assignment_forged_by_third_party.note: An unrelated third party (`imposter`, neither issuer nor performer) signs an Assignment for wo1 naming `performer`. Same shape as assignment_forged_by_performer: cryptographically valid, authorship-invalid. MUST be rejected with ERR_NOT_ISSUER.

progress1.author: performer
progress1.note: The assigned performer reports the `started` lifecycle state on wo1.

attestation1.author: attestor
attestation1.note: A completing Attestation for wo1, subject `performer`, carrying a handoff-code proof (§10.2-10.3, kind=0) whose `code` is "4417" — see the `proof` group vectors for the commitment check.

vectors.description: Shortest-form unsigned integer encoding at the CBOR argument-width boundary (0).
Shortest-form unsigned integer encoding at the CBOR argument-width boundary (1).
Shortest-form unsigned integer encoding at the CBOR argument-width boundary (23).
Shortest-form unsigned integer encoding at the CBOR argument-width boundary (24).
Shortest-form unsigned integer encoding at the CBOR argument-width boundary (255).
Shortest-form unsigned integer encoding at the CBOR argument-width boundary (256).
Shortest-form unsigned integer encoding at the CBOR argument-width boundary (65535).
Shortest-form unsigned integer encoding at the CBOR argument-width boundary (65536).
Shortest-form unsigned integer encoding at the CBOR argument-width boundary (4294967295).
Shortest-form unsigned integer encoding at the CBOR argument-width boundary (4294967296).
Shortest-form negative integer encoding (-1).
Shortest-form negative integer encoding (-24).
Shortest-form negative integer encoding (-25).
Shortest-form negative integer encoding (-300).
Boolean true.
Boolean false.

CBOR null (major 7, simple value 22) — the value an omitted-field slot decodes to if one is ever present explicitly; WRAP's own encoder omits MAY fields rather than nulling them (§4.1), but a decoder must still read one.
Empty text string.
Short text string.
Empty byte string.
Short byte string.
Empty definite-length array.
Definite-length array of three uints.
Empty map.
Map keys MUST be sorted in ascending bytewise order of their own encoded bytes (§4.1), not insertion order. This map is built with keys in the order 5,1,3 and must encode as 1,3,5.
Positive zero — exact in half precision.
Negative zero — sign bit must survive minimisation (§4.1: shortest form that preserves the *value*, and IEEE 754 -0.0 != 0.0 as a bit pattern).

1.0 — exact in half precision (2-byte form), NOT the 8-byte double an always-double encoder would emit. This is the exact interop trap fixed on this branch (see cbor.go's prior always-emit-0xfb behaviour).
1.5 — exact in half precision.
-2.0 — exact in half precision, sign handled.
100000.0 — exceeds half precision's max normal (~65504) but is exactly representable in single precision (4-byte form).

3.14 — an ordinary decimal fraction with no finite binary expansion; not exactly representable in half OR single precision, so it correctly stays the full 8-byte double. Minimisation must never round — only narrow when the narrower width is BIT-EXACT.
-33.9 — a realistic Place.lat value (§3.9); same non-exact-in-narrower-width reasoning as pi_approx, included because WGS84 coordinates are the field WRAP actually carries floats in.
Deterministic CBOR encoding of a WorkOrder (kind 0x01) with every documented field populated.
Deterministic CBOR encoding of an Offer (kind 0x02).
Deterministic CBOR encoding of a Bid (kind 0x03).
Deterministic CBOR encoding of an Assignment (kind 0x04).
Deterministic CBOR encoding of a Progress (kind 0x05).
Deterministic CBOR encoding of an Attestation (kind 0x06), including a nested Proof map.

id = 0x1e || BLAKE3-256(canonical_bytes), where canonical_bytes is the object's deterministic CBOR encoding with key 3 (id) and the signature both omitted (§4.3). Recompute over wo1's canonical_bytes and compare to the given id.
Same check as id-wo1, over a different object kind, to pin that id computation is kind-independent.

Structural property, not a byte check: canonical_bytes (the id's own preimage) never contains key 3 at all — id is derived FROM canonical_bytes, not carried inside it and then verified. Inspect wo1's canonical_bytes_hex and confirm no key-3 entry is present in the decoded map (its highest WorkOrder-scope key present is 15/`body`, or 14/`refs` here; the header keys present are 1,2,4,5 only — never 3).

preimage = "WRAP-v0/object" || 0x00 || canonical_bytes (§5.2). The domain tag is the 14 ASCII bytes "WRAP-v0/object" (no trailing NUL as part of the tag itself — the 0x00 terminator is a separate, additional byte), followed by exactly one 0x00 byte, followed by canonical_bytes verbatim. Verify signature_hex is a valid Ed25519 signature (RFC 8032) by author_pub_hex over exactly this preimage for wo1.
A correctly signed envelope decodes and its signature verifies against the claimed author.
Flipping the last byte of a validly signed envelope's signature must be DETECTED, not silently accepted.

wo1's canonical_bytes re-signed so that `author` (key 4) claims `imposter`'s public key, but the bytes are actually signed with `issuer`'s private key. The preimage covers the author field itself, so this signature does not verify against the key it claims — MUST be rejected with ERR_BAD_SIG, exactly like a bit-flipped signature, not accepted because "a valid Ed25519 signature exists somewhere".

canonical_bytes containing key 0 anywhere MUST be rejected with ERR_FORBIDDEN_KEY (§4.5), regardless of what else the object contains. This vector's map is {0:"boom", 1:0(v), 2:1(kind=WorkOrder), 4:<issuer pubkey>, 5:<ts>, 6:"x", 7:"y", 13:1(expires)} — otherwise a plausible-looking WorkOrder, poisoned by key 0.

The single byte value 1 encoded via the 2-byte form (major 0, additional info 24, then byte 0x01) instead of its 1-byte shortest form (0x01). Well-formed CBOR, not the canonical encoding of the integer 1. A receiver MUST reject this, not decode-then-re-encode-then-accept (§5.4 step 1) — repairing it would let two distinct byte strings share one recomputed id.

The same non-shortest-form-integer defect as reject-non-canonical-minimal, but inside a realistic object: `kind` (key 2, value 1) is encoded as 0x18 0x01 instead of 0x01, embedded in an otherwise well-formed WorkOrder-shaped canonical_bytes map. This is the shape the defect actually takes in practice — a single mis-minimised field inside a real message, not a bare top-level scalar.

An object whose envelope exceeds 65536 bytes MUST be rejected with ERR_TOO_LARGE (§4.6). This vector is a WorkOrder whose `detail` field alone is 70000 ASCII bytes, producing a >65KB envelope. KNOWN GAP: as of this branch, github.com/vul-os/propfix's internal/wrap.Decode performs no size check at all and accepts this envelope. This is a genuine, currently-unfixed conformance defect distinct from the float-encoding one this branch does fix — see the accompanying Go conformance-vector test for the live assertion of this gap.

AMBIGUOUS PER SPEC (see accompanying README / conformance report): §5.4 step 4 says to "recompute id and compare with key 3", implying key 3 is transmitted and checked against the recomputation. But §5.2-5.3 define canonical_bytes (the ONLY thing carried in the bare `[canonical_bytes, signature]` envelope, §5.3) as EXCLUDING key 3 by construction (§4.3: "canonical_bytes is exactly the bytes hashed for id ... the deterministic CBOR encoding of the object with key 3 and the signature omitted"). There is therefore no wire representation in the bare envelope binding that carries a claimed id to compare against the recomputed one — ERR_BAD_ID as literally described can only apply in a context where id is transmitted or claimed OUT OF BAND from canonical_bytes, e.g. a content-addressed fetch/store binding where a peer requests object `id=X` and what comes back does not hash to X. This vector documents that reading: constructing canonical_bytes for wo1, then asking "what if a peer claims this object's id is a different byte string Y" (Y = wo1's id with the last byte flipped).

v (key 1) = 1, a format version this document does not define (v0 only). MUST be ignored/rejected rather than best-effort interpreted (§4.2) — the object otherwise looks like a plausible future WorkOrder.
The WorkOrder's own issuer authoring its Assignment is the only valid case — MUST be accepted.

An Assignment authored by the PERFORMER (not the issuer) — cryptographically valid, authorship-invalid. §3.6/§5.5: MUST be rejected with ERR_NOT_ISSUER and MUST NOT enter local state, not merely be down-ranked or treated as a competing offer to merge against assignment_legit.
An Assignment authored by an unrelated third party (neither issuer nor performer) for wo1. Same rejection as authorship-assignment-by-performer-rejected.

Bid's authorship rule is 'any Principal the offer's pool admits' (§5.5), and pool admission is a social/out-of-band matter (§8.4), not something the wire-verification layer can check. Two bids on the same offer, from two UNRELATED keys (`performer` in bid1, `imposter` in bid_any_principal), must both decode and signature-verify with no authorship rejection.

Minting twice within the same wall-clock millisecond: max(now_ms,last_ms) == last_ms, so counter increments and unix_ms stays fixed (§7.2 Minting). First mint at ms=1784500000000 with a clean clock yields counter=0000; a second mint at the SAME now_ms=1784500000000 yields counter=0001, both stamps sharing unix_ms.
vectors.author: issuer

vectors.description: When now_ms strictly exceeds last_ms, counter resets to 0000 rather than continuing to increment (§7.2 Minting: 'If it equals last_ms, increment counter; otherwise reset counter to zero').
vectors.author: issuer

vectors.description: Observing a remote stamp advances the local clock to max(local, remote) BEFORE the next mint (§7.2 Observing), so a subsequent local mint sorts strictly after the observed remote stamp even though the local wall clock never reached that time. Local last_ms starts at 0 (clean clock); observe a remote stamp at ms=1784500001000 counter=0002 from `performer`; the next local mint (local wall clock frozen at 1784500000000, i.e. BEFORE the observed stamp) must still produce a stamp that sorts after the observed one.
vectors.author: issuer

vectors.description: A node whose wall clock steps backwards (e.g. RTC reset) MUST seed last_ms from the highest stamp it has already issued or observed, not from the (now-lower) wall clock, so it cannot mint a stamp that sorts below existing state (§7.2 Minting: 'A node MUST seed last_ms from the highest stamp it has already issued or observed'). Node has previously minted up to ms=1784500005000; its wall clock then reads 1784500000000 (5 seconds earlier than its own last mint). The next mint MUST NOT produce unix_ms < 1784500005000.
vectors.author: issuer

vectors.description: Two stamps with IDENTICAL unix_ms (1784500000000) and IDENTICAL counter (0000), differing only in author_hex, are ordered by ASCENDING byte comparison of author_hex (§7.3). This is §15.3 item 1, 'the most likely divergence in the whole document'. The two author keys here (tieA, tieB) are real Ed25519 public keys derived from fixed seeds (0x66..0x66 and 0x77..0x77) so the comparison is over genuine 32-byte keys, not synthetic test strings.

expect.MUST_NOT: introduce a node identifier into the ordering (§7.3, final sentence)

vectors.description: Because unix_ms is zero-padded to 13 digits and counter to 4 hex digits, the ENTIRE ts value — not just the tie-break — is a lexically sortable string (§7.2): ordinary string/byte comparison of the full ts is the total order, with no field parsing required. This vector is a smoke test of that property using wo1's own ts.

WRAP state is a set of signed objects; merging two replicas is set union (§7.1). Replica A holds {wo1, offer1}; replica B holds {wo1, bid1}. Their merge (union) is {wo1, offer1, bid1} — order and count of exchange rounds do not matter (commutative, associative, idempotent).
Delivering the SAME object (wo1) to a replica multiple times must not change the resulting state — union of a set with itself is itself (§7.1: 'a duplicate delivery is free rather than harmful').

The object set {wo1, offer1, bid1, assignment_legit, progress1} delivered to replica A in creation order, and to replica B in REVERSE order, must converge to byte-identical state — union is order-independent (§7.1, §7.4).

expect.state_root_hex: computed as 0x1e || BLAKE3-256(id_1 || id_2 || ... || id_n), ids sorted ASCENDING by byte value first (§7.4) — not creation order, not delivery order

vectors.description: Assignment merges by last-writer-wins on ts AMONG OBJECTS BY THE AUTHORIZED AUTHOR ONLY (§7.1 table) — assignment_forged_by_performer and assignment_forged_by_third_party MUST NOT participate in this LWW comparison at all, regardless of their ts, because they fail authorship (§5.5) before merge ever sees them. Only assignment_legit is eligible, so it wins trivially (it is the only valid Assignment for wo1 in this object set).
Given only {wo1} (no Offer, no Assignment, now < wo1.expires), the computed state is `issued` (§6.3 rule 5).
Given {wo1, offer1} (an Offer exists, no Assignment, now < expires), the computed state is `offered` (§6.3 rule 4).

Given {wo1, offer1, bid1, assignment_legit} with no Progress yet, the computed state is `assigned`, the default when a valid non-revoked Assignment exists and no Progress state is reachable beyond it yet (§6.3 rule 2).
Adding progress1 (state=`started`) on top of fold-assigned's object set advances the computed state to `started` — the highest-ts Progress state reachable from `assigned` (§6.3 rule 2).

A Progress reporting `completed` when NO Assignment exists at all for wo1 is unreachable from the implied `issued`/`offered` state and MUST be discarded (not applied, not erroring, MAY be logged) rather than jumping the state machine (§6.3, final paragraph: 'a performer cannot report completed on a work order they were never assigned'). Object set: {wo1, offer1} plus a synthetic Progress(order=wo1, state="completed") authored by `performer`, who holds no Assignment for wo1 in this set.

wo1.expires = 1900000000. Given only {wo1} (no Offer, no Assignment) and now = 1950000000 (past expires, no message exchanged of any kind), the computed state is `expired` (§6.2, §6.3 rule 3) — computed locally and identically by every replica the instant their own clock passes expires, with zero coordination.
Same object set, now = 1850000000 (before wo1.expires = 1900000000): state is NOT expired.

wo1 + a valid Assignment, now well past wo1.expires: an assigned work order does NOT transition to `expired` even after the expiry timestamp passes — §6.3's rule order checks Assignment (rule 2) strictly before the expiry check (rule 3), and expires only gates whether a NEW Bid or Assignment may be created (§3.3), not whether an already-assigned order retroactively expires.

An object of kind 0x55 (undefined; the 0x40-0x7f profile range and 0x80+ future-core range are both silently-ignore ranges, §3.1, §4.4) MUST still decode and signature-verify successfully — rejection or erroring at the decode/verify layer is itself non-conformant. A dispatcher over the six known kinds simply has no matching branch and does nothing further; it is never told the object was invalid, because it was not.

wo2_unknown_field carries key 20 (inside the 16-31 core-reserved range, §4.5), which this document does not define. Decoding it, then re-encoding the DECODED value (not merely retransmitting the original bytes verbatim) MUST reproduce byte-identical canonical_bytes — dropping key 20 on the round trip would silently invalidate the object's own signature for every downstream relay (§4.4: 'doing so would invalidate the signature and silently corrupt the object for everyone downstream'). This is §15.3 item 2, called out as a failure mode whose reporter will not be the implementation that dropped the field.

A WorkOrder whose `profile` (key 6) is a string this implementation has no profile definition for — e.g. `"example.org:towing/v0"` (§12.5's namespacing convention for undefined profiles) — MUST still be verified, stored, relayed and merged exactly like any other WorkOrder (§4.4, §12.1: 'An implementation that does not recognise a profile MUST still be able to verify, store, relay and merge its objects'). Only RENDERING it meaningfully is unavailable. `profile` is an ordinary tstr field with no registry validation at the wire layer, so this is not a special code path — it is the ABSENCE of one, which is the point.

Handoff-code fulfilment (§10.2-10.3): the issuer publishes commit = BLAKE3-256(code || order_id) in the Assignment's `terms` BEFORE handoff; the completing Attestation later carries the plaintext code in its `proof` map (kind=0). Any party recomputes BLAKE3-256(code || order_id) and checks it against the published commitment. Here code="4417" (ASCII bytes, not decoded as a number) and order_id = wo1's id (33 raw bytes, the 0x1e-prefixed multihash form) — commit = BLAKE3-256("4417" || wo1.id).

The same construction with a WRONG code ("9999" instead of the actual "4417") produces a DIFFERENT commit hash, which MUST NOT match the one published in the Assignment — this is the negative case that makes the positive case meaningful (a verifier that accepts any hash isn't verifying anything).
